segments.title: The Royal Navy's new postwar carrier fleet
segments.summary: Meet the Centaur- and Audacious-class carriers that gave the Royal Navy a largely new carrier force by the Suez Crisis.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke surveys the Centaur-class ships Centaur, Albion, Bulwark, and Hermes alongside the larger Eagle and Ark Royal. Their construction began during the Second World War, but commissioning dates in the 1950s made most of them new operational ships at Suez. The force was also reinforced by the return of HMS Victorious. This scale of postwar commissioning matters because it shows that Britain entered the early jet era with substantial carrier capacity rather than relying only on worn wartime hulls.

evidence.note: The transcript identifies the Centaur- and Audacious-class ships, their commissioning dates, and the six post-1950 carrier commissions.

segments.title: Angled decks replaced a rubber landing experiment
segments.summary: See how British carrier experiments ranged from undercarriage-free rubber landings to the practical angled flight deck.
segments.body: British engineers explored landing aircraft without undercarriages onto a rubberized deck in hopes of spreading impact loads and reducing airframe stress. Eric Brown's trials demonstrated the danger and awkwardness of that approach, while the angled flight deck became the durable solution. Dr. Clarke also explains that the angled deck helped balance dense machinery and exhaust trunking on the starboard side, not merely the visible island. The design therefore served both flight operations and ship weight management.

evidence.note: The transcript contrasts rubberized undercarriage-free landings with angled decks and explains the starboard machinery-weight issue.

segments.title: Britain's five-carrier Suez force
segments.summary: The Suez operation combined three strike carriers with two helicopter carriers while Britain maintained commitments elsewhere.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke describes a battle group centered on five carriers: three strike carriers and two Colossus-class ships serving in the amphibious helicopter role. Britain could assemble that concentration while continuing other global commitments, which illustrates the depth of its mid-1950s fleet. The strike carriers were recent commissions, comparable in relative freshness to the Colossus-class ships used during Korea. Suez thus demonstrates both carrier mass and the growing division between strike and amphibious aviation missions.

evidence.note: The transcript counts five carriers, distinguishes three strike ships from two amphibious carriers, and stresses their newness.

segments.title: A specialized Suez carrier air group
segments.summary: Skyraider warning aircraft, Wyvern attack planes, and new Sea Hawk jets show how quickly Royal Navy carrier aviation changed after Korea.
segments.body: The air group had changed markedly within only a few years. Skyraiders carried airborne early-warning radar because their endurance and payload suited the mission, while Westland Wyverns provided day-and-night attack capability and Sea Hawks supplied modern jet performance. Nearly the entire combination was British, with the American Skyraider as the conspicuous exception. The mix shows an air group becoming a collection of specialized platforms rather than interchangeable fighters.

evidence.note: The transcript introduces the Skyraider AEW, Wyvern attack aircraft, and newly introduced Sea Hawk.

segments.title: The Wyvern as a turboprop insurance policy
segments.summary: The Royal Navy retained the powerful Wyvern so its strike force did not depend entirely on immature jet technology.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke presents the Westland Wyvern as an insurance policy during a rapid propulsion transition. Its turboprop powerplant offered reliability, long range, and a useful load of bombs or mines, even though it was slower than a jet. Keeping it in service avoided placing every carrier-attack requirement in one technological basket. The decision illustrates how navies manage innovation risk by overlapping mature and emerging systems.

evidence.note: The transcript calls the Wyvern an insurance policy and describes its reliability, range, armament, and payload.

segments.title: The Sea Venom brought all-weather jet interception
segments.summary: The two-seat Sea Venom combined jet speed, heavy cannon armament, and day-and-night carrier operations.
segments.body: The Sea Venom filled a role distinct from the single-seat Sea Hawk by carrying two crew and supporting all-weather operations. It combined four 20 mm cannon with rockets or bombs and approached 576 miles per hour at sea level. Some examples also served in electronic-warfare duties, extending the type beyond interception and attack. Its capabilities show how early carrier jets were already separating into specialized operational roles.

evidence.note: The transcript gives the Sea Venom's crew, speed, armament, all-weather status, and electronic-warfare use.

segments.title: Soviet cruisers were designed to disperse NATO strength
segments.summary: Soviet surface raiders threatened distant sea lanes partly to pull NATO carriers and escorts away from the North Atlantic and Mediterranean.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke explains the strategic logic behind Soviet cruiser construction under Stalin. Ships operating as distant raiders could conduct peacetime presence missions and, in war, force NATO to divert scarce forces from Russia's Atlantic and Mediterranean approaches. Hunting one cruiser in the South Atlantic might consume a carrier group with its aircraft and escorts; several raiders would multiply the burden. The cruiser's value therefore lay as much in forcing enemy dispersion as in the damage it might inflict directly.

evidence.note: The transcript explains Soviet raider strategy and estimates the carrier-group effort required to hunt cruisers in distant waters.

segments.title: Carrier missions expanded beyond the Suez strike
segments.summary: Suez repeated wartime strike patterns, but the Soviet threat pushed carriers toward air defense, reconnaissance, long-range attack, and anti-submarine warfare.
segments.body: Suez demonstrated an effective strike mission, yet Dr. Clarke argues that it was not by itself a revolutionary tactical event. The Royal Navy was simultaneously planning for combat air patrol, fleet air defense, long-range strike, reconnaissance, and the Soviet submarine threat. The Fairey Gannet emerged within that widening mission set as an anti-submarine aircraft. Early jet-age carrier doctrine therefore evolved through a broader sensor-and-defense problem, not simply faster bombing aircraft.

evidence.note: The transcript contrasts Suez strike experience with air patrol, air defense, long-range strike, reconnaissance, and the Fairey Gannet's ASW role.
